14: hinei haalmah harah veyoledet ben veqarat shemo immanuel (hinnēh hāʿalmāh hārāh wĕyōledet bēn wĕqārāt šĕmô ʿimmānûʾēl): 'Behold the young woman is with child and shall bear a son and shall call his name Immanuel.' The philological crux: almah (young woman) vs. the LXX parthenos (virgin). Almah means 'young woman of marriageable age' — not a technical term for biological virginity (which is betulah). However, in context an unmarried almah would normally be a virgin; Matthew's use of LXX parthenos sees the deeper Christological fulfillment where the historical sign (birth within a year to a young woman in Ahaz's time) is transcended by the ultimate Immanuel born without a human father — the literal 'God with us.'
1: Historical context: the Syro-Ephraimitic War (ca. 735 BCE). Reṣîn melek ʾărām wûpĕqaḥ ben-Rĕmalyāhû melek yiśrāʾēl = Rezin of Syria and Pekah of Israel — the northern coalition forcing Judah (under Ahaz) to join their anti-Assyrian alliance. wĕlōʾ yāḵĕlû lĕhillāḥēm ʿālāyw = "but they were not able to fight against it" — despite threatening Jerusalem, the attack failed. Isaiah's task (vv3-9): assure Ahaz that this threat will not succeed.
2: wayyānāʿ lĕḇāḇô ûlĕḇab ʿammô kĕnôaʿ ʿăṣê-yaʿar = "his heart and the heart of his people shook like trees of the forest before the wind." Nûaʿ (H5128) = to shake, totter. The collective panic is portrayed with the simile of wind-shaken trees — the same verb (nûaʿ) appears in 10:34 when YHWH shakes the forest of Assyrian pride. The instrument of terror will itself be shaken.
3: YHWH sends Isaiah and šĕʾār yāšûḇ bĕnô = "his son Shear-jashub" — the name means "a remnant will return." The child's presence is a walking sign-act: there will be a remnant, but only a remnant. Isaiah meets Ahaz biqṣēh tĕʿālat habĕrēkâ hāʿelyônâ = "at the end of the conduit of the upper pool" — an engineering landmark related to Jerusalem's water supply, possibly where Hezekiah later built his tunnel (2 Kgs 20:20). The mundane location for a divine message is characteristically prophetic.
4: hiššāmer wĕhašqēṭ ʾal-tîrāʾ = "Take heed, be quiet, do not fear" — three imperatives of the prophetic assurance formula. ûlĕḇāḇĕkā ʾal-yirk = "do not let your heart grow soft (rākaḵ)" — rākaḵ = to be timid (opposite of firm). mišĕnê zanĕḇôt hāʾûdîm hāʿăšēnîm hāʾēlleh = "before these two smoldering (ʿāšēn) stumps of firebrands (ʾûd)" — Rezin and Pekah are not roaring lions but dying embers, stumps of old torches with more smoke than fire.
5: yaʿan kî yāʿaṣ ʿālêkā ʾărām rāʿâ = "because Syria has planned evil against you." Eprayim ûben-Rĕmalyāhû = Ephraim (northern Israel) and "the son of Remaliah" — the latter used 5 times in this section instead of Pekah's name; a subtle dismissal of his royal dignity (cf. v9: "the head of Ephraim is the son of Remaliah").
6: naʿăleh ḇiyĕhûdâ ûnĕqîṣennâ = "Let us go up against Judah and terrorize it." The coalition plans to replace Ahaz with a puppet king, ben-ṭāḇĕʾal = "son of Tabeel" — a non-Davidic figure. The threat is not merely military conquest but termination of the Davidic dynasty — which is why the Immanuel promise matters: the house of David will not be extinguished.
7: kōh ʾāmar ʾădōnāy YHWH lōʾ tāqûm wĕlōʾ tihyeh = "Thus says the Lord YHWH: It shall not stand, and it shall not come to pass." Qûm = to arise/stand (of a plan becoming reality). The conspiracy against the Davidic house will fail because YHWH's promise to David's line (2 Sam 7; Ps 89) is irrevocable.
8: kî rōʾš ʾărām Dammāśeq wĕrōʾš Dammāśeq Reṣîn = "For the head of Syria is Damascus, and the head of Damascus is Rezin" — the political argument: the coalition's strength is limited by geography; Damascus is merely its capital and Rezin merely its king. wĕʾôd šiššîm wĕḥāmēš šānâ yēḥat ʾEprayim mēʿām = "and within sixty-five years Ephraim will be shattered from being a people" — fulfilled in stages: 722 BCE (Sargon II's deportation) and ca. 670 BCE (Esarhaddon's repopulation).
9:
wĕrōʾš ʾEprayim Šōmrôn wĕrōʾš Šōmrôn ben-Rĕmalyāhû = "the head of Ephraim is Samaria and the head of Samaria is the son of Remaliah" — Pekah deflated to mere "son of Remaliah," a regional governor at best.

ʾim lōʾ taʾămînû kî lōʾ tēʾāmēnû = "If you will not believe (taʾămînû), surely you shall not be established (tēʾāmēnû)" — the famous Hebrew wordplay on ʾāman (H539): the root of both "believe/trust" and "be established/firm." Faith in YHWH's word is the basis of political and personal stability; without the former the latter cannot exist. This verse is crucial for NT understanding of faith-as-foundation (cf. Rom 1:17; Heb 11:1: pistis as the "substance/foundation" of things hoped for).
10: wayyôsep YHWH dabbēr ʾel-ʾāḥāz = "and YHWH spoke again to Ahaz" — YHWH presses past Ahaz's silence or evasion; the offer of a sign (ʾôt) is a direct invitation to trust. The prophetic word pursues even the unwilling king.
11: šĕʾal-lĕkā ʾôt mēʿim YHWH ʾĕlōhêkā = "Ask for a sign (ʾôt) from YHWH your God." hămēq šĕʾālâ ô hāgbēah lĕmāʿĕlâ = "as deep as Sheol or as high as the heights above" — the extremes of the cosmos are offered. YHWH's generosity will be contrasted with Ahaz's refusal.
12: wāyyōʾmer ʾāḥāz lōʾ-ʾešʾal wĕlōʾ-ʾănasseh ʾet-YHWH = "I will not ask and I will not put YHWH to the test." Ahaz quotes Deut 6:16 piously — but this is evasion, not faithfulness. He has already decided to seek the Assyrian alliance (2 Kgs 16:7-8) rather than trust YHWH. The refusal of the sign is the refusal of faith, with Deuteronomic language as cover.
13: wāyyōʾmer šimʿû-nāʾ bêt Dāwid = "Hear then, O house of David" — Isaiah addresses not just Ahaz but the entire Davidic dynasty; the sign will be for the royal house and its ultimate future. hĕmeʿaṭ mikkĕm halĕʾôt ʾănāšîm kî talĕʾû gam ʾet-ʾĕlōhāy = "Is it too little for you to weary men, that you weary my God also?" — Ahaz has exhausted both Isaiah's patience and YHWH's.
15: ḥemʾâ ûdĕḇaš yōʾkēl = "Curds (ḥemʾâ) and honey (dĕḇaš) he shall eat." Canaan was "flowing with milk and honey" (Exod 3:8), but these subsistence foods in the context of invasion mean the child will grow up in a reduced, war-impoverished land. Curds-and-honey = the food of disrupted agriculture when only pastoral herding and wild honey remain. lĕdaʿtô lĕmāʾōs bārāʿ ûḇāḥôr baṭṭôḇ = "by the time he knows to reject the evil and choose the good" — moral discernment as developmental marker; the sign will be fulfilled within a child's growing-up span.
16: kî bĕṭerem yēdaʿ hannaʿar mĕʾōs bārāʿ ûḇāḥōr baṭṭôḇ = "for before the child knows to refuse the evil and choose the good, the land whose two kings you dread will be deserted." Near-fulfillment: both Rezin and Pekah were defeated (Rezin by 732 BCE, Pekah assassinated ca. 731 BCE) within years — within a child's early development. Structure of prophetic fulfillment: partial near-fulfillment (Isaiah's era) → ultimate fulfillment (incarnation).
17: yāḇîʾ YHWH ʿālêkā... yāmîm ʾăšer lōʾ-bāʾû... ʾēt melek ʾAššûr = "YHWH will bring upon you days not seen since Ephraim separated from Judah — the king of Assyria." The judgment pivots: Ahaz thought Assyria would save him (2 Kgs 16:7); YHWH warns that the Assyrian he courts will become Judah's greatest threat. The very instrument Ahaz seeks for protection becomes the instrument of punishment for Ahaz's unfaith.
18: yišrōq YHWH laḏḏĕḇôrâ = "YHWH will whistle for the fly... and the bee in the land of Assyria" — two great powers, both summoned by YHWH's whistle (cf. 5:26). They will cover the land like insect swarms — overwhelming by sheer density. Ahaz's political calculus fails to account for YHWH's total sovereignty over both powers simultaneously.
19: ûḇāʾû wĕnāḥû kullām = "and they shall come and settle everywhere" — in steep ravines (naḥălê bĕṭṭôt), rock crevices (nĕqîqê hassĕlāʿîm), thorn-bushes (nĕʿāṣôṣ), and watered pastures (nĕhālōlîm). No hiding place is exempt; every corner of the land is reached by the invaders.
20: yĕgallaḥ ʾădōnāy bĕtaʿar haśśĕkîrâ... bĕmelek ʾAššûr = "the Lord will shave with a razor that is hired — the king of Assyria." The shaving metaphor describes comprehensive defeat/humiliation: the head, the beard, the hair of the legs removed. Šĕʿar hārāglayim = "hair of the feet/legs" = euphemism for pubic hair — the shame is total. Assyria is YHWH's barber for a disgraced Judah.
21: yĕḥayyeh ʾîš ʿeglat bāqār ûštê ṣōʾn = "a man will keep alive a young cow and two sheep" — the pastoral image of a reduced post-invasion economy. The contrast with pre-invasion prosperity (vineyards, grain fields) is stark: survival subsistence farming replaces commercial agriculture.
22: wĕhāyâ mērōḇ ʿăśôt ḥālāḇ yōʾkal ḥemʾâ = "because of the abundance of milk they produce, he will eat curds" — not prosperity but the natural produce of a reduced herd when the complex agricultural economy has been destroyed. Curds and honey = pastoral survivor diet in a depopulated landscape (cf. v15).
23: kol-māqôm ʾăšer yihyeh-šām ʾelep gepen bĕ-ʾelep kesep lašāmîr wāšît yihyeh = "every place where there were a thousand vines worth a thousand pieces of silver will become briars and thorns" — the expensive vineyard (1,000 vines = 1,000 shekels) returns to šāmîr wāšît (the Isaiah-pair for cursed wilderness, cf. 5:6). Economic collapse total.
24: baḥiṣṣîm ûḇaqqeśet yāḇôʾ šāmmâ = "with arrows and bow one will come there" — the cultivated land becomes hunting ground; former vineyards become wilderness for archers. Inversion of cultivation to wilderness is the judgment pattern of Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy 28 enacted.
25: wĕkol-hehārîm ʾăšer bammĕʿdēr yēʿādērûn lōʾ-tāḇôʾ šāmmâ yirʾat šāmîr wāšît = "all the hills that were cultivated with a hoe — you will not come there for fear of briars and thorns." The terraced hillside vineyards, centuries of labor, revert to thorn-wilderness. wĕhāyâ lĕmišlaḥ šôr ûlĕmirmas śeh = "but they will be a place for oxen to roam and for sheep to tread" — subsistence pastoralism replaces commercial viticulture. The judgment of ch5 (the vineyard woes) is now fully enacted.
